faq.a: Both, ideally. Other expats are easier to connect with initially and understand your situation. Locals provide roots, deeper cultural insight, and tend to be more stable when expats come and go. Build both kinds of connection over time.

faq.q: I have been abroad for a year and still feel lonely. What am I doing wrong?
faq.a: Probably nothing wrong, but possibly not enough repetition. Ask yourself: am I showing up to the same places consistently? Am I following up after good conversations? Am I suggesting specific things rather than waiting to be included? Loneliness this far in is usually a sign that the connection exists but the maintenance is missing.

faq.q: How do I maintain friendships back home while building new ones abroad?
faq.a: Use the same principles: short, regular contact beats infrequent long catch-ups. A quick message, a shared link, a brief voice note. Your home friendships will not maintain themselves, but they also do not require a lot if you keep showing up in small ways.

guides.title: How to Know if Someone Wants to Be Your Friend | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: Reading the signals of mutual interest is one of the hardest parts of adult friendship. A practical guide to recognizing when a connection has real potential.
guides.h1: How to know if someone wants to be your friend
guides.lead: The question of whether someone actually wants to be your friend is one most adults never ask out loud, but almost everyone wonders. The signals are usually there. Most people just do not know what to look for.
guides.intro: You can tell whether someone is interested in friendship by watching what they do after a good conversation: do they follow up, engage in your messages, suggest seeing each other again, and bring genuine attention to what you share? Those behaviors are more reliable signals than how warm they seemed in the moment.
guides.pullQuote: Being liked and being chosen are different things. The first is a moment. The second is a pattern. Watch what someone does when there is no social pressure to be warm.

sections.h2: The difference between being liked and being chosen
paragraphs: Someone can genuinely like you, enjoy your company, and find your conversation interesting without being in a position to invest in a friendship. Life circumstances, energy levels, existing social commitments, and timing all play a role.

Being liked is necessary but not sufficient for friendship. What matters is whether someone is available, willing, and taking small steps to keep the connection alive. Those behaviors are the signal that a friendship is possible.

sections.h2: Signs someone is interested in becoming friends
paragraphs: They follow up after meeting. Even a short message that says they enjoyed talking to you is a meaningful signal. Most people who are not interested simply do not follow up.
They remember things you told them and bring them up in later conversations. This shows they were paying attention and that you occupy some space in their thinking.
They suggest plans or say yes to yours with genuine enthusiasm and follow-through. Repeated cancellations or vague responses to invitations are a signal that the interest is limited.
They share something personal with you, an opinion, a difficulty, something beyond surface-level small talk. That kind of openness usually signals trust and a genuine interest in building something.
sections.quote: What separates an acquaintance from a friend is usually not chemistry. It is someone being willing to suggest the next time.

cultureRef.body: In Ted Lasso, Ted brings Rebecca a tin of shortbread biscuits every morning. Not once. Every morning. Rebecca is cold and gives him nothing in return for weeks. But she keeps the tin. She never tells him to stop. That pattern tells him something. In real life, the signals work the same way. Someone who keeps showing up in small ways, remembering the detail from last week, suggesting the specific plan, is doing the equivalent of bringing the biscuits.

sections.h2: Signs the connection may not go further
paragraphs: They are warm in the moment but never follow up. Some people are genuinely friendly in social settings and equally genuinely not investing in new friendships. The warmth is real, but the bandwidth is not there.
They agree to plans but cancel often, or plans never quite materialize. This can mean they like you but do not have capacity, or simply that the friendship is not a priority for them.
Conversations feel one-sided, with you asking questions and them not reciprocating genuine curiosity about you. Interest in a potential friendship usually shows up as mutual curiosity.

sections.h2: Do not over-read one interaction
paragraphs: A great first conversation is a good sign, not a guarantee. Many good conversations happen between people who never become friends because no one follows up, or because the circumstances do not create enough repeated contact.

The pattern that matters is over multiple interactions. Are they engaging consistently? Are they adding energy to the connection or leaving you to do all the work? That pattern over time is more reliable than any single exchange.

Research on social perception consistently shows that people are poor judges of another person's interest after a single interaction, and that they tend to underestimate it rather than overestimate it. This connects to the "liking gap" documented by researchers at Yale: both parties in a conversation typically believe the interaction mattered more to them than to the other. The pattern that matters is behavioral and repeated. Consistent follow-through, genuine curiosity, and repeated initiation are far more reliable signals of interest than any reading of how one conversation felt.

sections.h2: What to do with the uncertainty
paragraphs: The most practical response to uncertainty is to make a low-stakes move and read the response. Send a short follow-up, suggest coffee, share something interesting. If the other person responds with warmth and engagement, the interest is likely mutual.

If the response is lukewarm or absent, you have your answer without having invested much. Most adult friendship uncertainty resolves itself through small tests, not through trying to decode signals before taking any action. Once the signal is there, the next move is simple. See how to follow up after meeting someone.

faq.q: How can I tell if someone genuinely wants to be friends or is just being polite?
faq.a: Watch what happens after the conversation: do they follow up, engage your messages, suggest plans, and remember details from what you shared? Politeness stays surface-level. Genuine interest takes small steps toward more contact.

faq.q: Someone seems friendly every time we meet but never initiates. Does that mean they are not interested?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Many people are interested in a friendship but are waiting for someone else to make the first move. Consistent warmth at every encounter plus responsiveness to your invitations is a reasonable signal of interest, even without spontaneous initiation.

faq.q: How many times should I reach out before accepting that someone is not interested?
faq.a: Try two or three genuine invitations spread over a few weeks. If none of them lead to a plan or a warm response, the interest is probably not there, at least not right now. Pull back without resentment. Circumstances change.

faq.q: What if I am unsure whether my interest is mutual?
faq.a: Make a small move and read the response. Suggest something specific and low-stakes. A yes with follow-through signals interest. A vague answer or a no with no counter-suggestion tells you what you need to know without either of you having to say it explicitly.

faq.q: Someone I thought was becoming a friend suddenly went quiet. What happened?
faq.a: Many things can interrupt an emerging friendship: a demanding period at work, a relationship change, a health issue, or simply a shift in priorities. One period of quiet is not necessarily a signal that the friendship is over. A simple check-in message can reopen contact without any pressure.

guides.authorNote: This is one of the most common uncertainties I work through with people: whether someone is genuinely interested or just being polite. The signals are almost always there. You just have to know what to look for, and trust what you see rather than waiting for complete certainty before making any move.

guides.title: What Is Friendship Coaching? | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: Friendship coaching isn't therapy. It is a step-by-step strategy for your social life, like a personal trainer for your friendships.
guides.h1: What is friendship coaching?
guides.lead: Friendship coaching is practical support for adults who want help navigating the real challenges of adult social life: meeting people, following up, reconnecting, and keeping friendships alive. It is not therapy. It is not crisis support. It is a structured way to turn good intentions into action.
guides.intro: Friendship coaching is a form of practical coaching that focuses on one specific area of life: the relationships we call friendships. It helps adults identify what is getting in the way of the connections they want, and build the specific habits and actions that allow those connections to develop.
guides.pullQuote: Friendship coaching is not about becoming more likeable. It is about closing the gap between knowing what you want from your social life and actually doing something about it.

sections.h2: What friendship coaching actually involves
paragraphs: In a friendship coaching session, you look honestly at your current friendship life: who is in it, what feels frustrating, what you want more of, and what is in the way. Then you identify specific, real-life actions: who to reach out to, what to say, what context to invest in, what patterns to change.
After each session, you leave with one concrete friendship action to try before the next. Not homework in the abstract sense, but one specific move that fits your actual life.
The work is practical, forward-looking, and grounded in what is happening right now, not in theory or diagnosis.

Research on behavioral goal-setting consistently shows that people are significantly more likely to follow through on a social intention when it is translated into one specific, concrete action with a clear timeframe, rather than remaining at the level of general resolve. That principle is what friendship coaching is built on: not insight about what you should do, but clarity about what you will do before the next session.

sections.h2: What friendship coaching is not
paragraphs: Friendship coaching is not therapy. It does not treat mental health conditions, provide a diagnosis, address trauma, or offer crisis support. If you are struggling with depression, severe anxiety, or thoughts of harming yourself, please seek support from a qualified mental health professional.

It is also not a social skills training program, a script-writing service, or a promise that specific people will want to be your friend. No coach can guarantee that another person will reciprocate connection. What coaching can do is help you act more clearly and consistently on the connections that have real potential.

sections.h2: Who friendship coaching is for
paragraphs: Friendship coaching tends to be most useful for adults who are going through a transition: a move to a new city, a job change, remote work, new parenthood, a life phase where their existing friendships have faded, or a period of building connection after a long stretch of isolation.

It also helps people who have the social opportunities but struggle with the follow-through: they meet people they like, have good conversations, and then nothing happens. Or they have people in their lives they care about but have been meaning to reconnect with for months.

If you are curious whether this applies to you, the free 7-day Friendship Challenge is a practical starting point. It helps you see your friendship life clearly before deciding whether coaching is the right next step.

sections.h2: How The Friendship Practice works
paragraphs: The Friendship Practice is a private 4-session coaching package offered by Carole Stromboni. Sessions are 45 to 60 minutes and usually take place on Google Meet.

Each session focuses on one specific friendship situation: a reconnection you want to attempt, a new connection you want to deepen, a context you want to invest in, or a pattern you want to change. You leave with a concrete action step to try before the next session.

The entry point is the free 7-day Friendship Challenge. It is a 7-day reflection sequence, available as both a browser-based tracker and a daily email series, that helps you map your friendship life before trying to change it.
sections.quote: You do not need a more charismatic personality. You need a more deliberate practice.

sections.h2: Is friendship coaching right for you?
paragraphs: If you find yourself thinking about friendship often but not acting on those thoughts, or if you have been meaning to reach out, reconnect, or invest in a new connection and keep postponing it, coaching can help you close that gap.

The goal is not a social transformation. It is one clearer step, then another, until the friendship life you want starts to take actual shape. To understand why adult friendship is structurally harder than it used to be, start with why making friends as an adult is hard.

faq.q: What is friendship coaching?
faq.a: Friendship coaching is practical support for adults who want to make new friends, reconnect with old ones, or keep existing friendships alive. It focuses on specific actions, real-life situations, and follow-through, not on theory or diagnosis.

faq.q: Is friendship coaching the same as therapy?
faq.a: No. Friendship coaching is practical coaching, not therapy, mental health care, or crisis support. It does not treat mental health conditions or provide diagnoses. If you are struggling with something beyond the practical, please seek support from a qualified mental health professional.

faq.q: Who is friendship coaching for?
faq.a: Adults who want practical help making new friends, reconnecting with old ones, or deepening current friendships. It is particularly useful during life transitions: moving to a new city, starting remote work, rebuilding a social life after a difficult period.

faq.q: Does friendship coaching guarantee that I will make friends?
faq.a: No. No coach can guarantee that specific people will want to be your friend. What coaching can do is help you act more clearly and consistently on the connections that have real potential, so that more of those connections actually develop.

faq.q: How is The Friendship Practice different from a self-help book?
faq.a: A book gives you general information. Coaching applies that information to your specific situation: your people, your patterns, your real-life obstacles, and your next concrete step. The difference is between understanding something in theory and actually doing something about it.

faq.q: How do I know if I am ready for friendship coaching?
faq.a: Start with the free 7-day Friendship Challenge. It helps you look clearly at your friendship life before deciding whether coaching is the right next step. If after the challenge you want support applying what you noticed to one real friendship situation, coaching is a natural next step.

guides.title: How to Make Friends as a New Parent | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: New parenthood is isolating, and polite coffee dates aren't the answer. Here is how to find other parents who get it and build real connections fast.
guides.h1: How to make friends as a new parent
guides.lead: New parenthood is one of the loneliest periods in adult life, not because it lacks people, but because everyone is overwhelmed and nobody has energy left for the work of making friendship happen. The opportunity is there. The structure is not.
guides.intro: Making friends as a new parent comes down to finding other people in the same season of life and creating enough repeated contact that the connection has somewhere to go. You do not need deep conversations or long evenings. You need to show up in the same place more than once, and do something small when the moment is warm.
guides.authorNote: New parents surrounded by potential friends at baby groups and playgrounds still feel entirely alone six months later, because nobody had the bandwidth to follow up. The window is short and the energy is low. That is exactly why you have to make the move deliberately.
guides.pullQuote: There is almost no lonelier time in adult life than new parenthood. And almost no time when the potential for new friendship is more concentrated. The gap between those two facts is small and almost entirely about follow-through.

sections.h2: You are not imagining how hard it is
paragraphs: New parenthood disrupts friendship on every level: sleep deprivation, identity shift, the loss of the routines that used to sustain your social life, and the slow disappearance of people who are in a different life phase.
The people who were close to you before may not know what to do with the new version of your life. The people who would understand it are strangers you have not yet met.
This is structural, not personal. Understanding that helps. You are not failing at friendship. You are trying to build it in one of the hardest conditions adults face.

The Gottman Institute has studied the transition to parenthood across decades of research. They found that 67 percent of new parents experience a significant drop in relationship satisfaction in the first three years after a baby. One of the main drivers is the collapse of the social infrastructure that used to support both partners. Their research is clear: the social isolation of new parenthood does not correct itself. It is a structural problem that requires deliberate attention. (The Gottman Institute)

Time-use data from the Pew Research Center on Modern Parenthood shows that both parents lose discretionary social time after a baby, but in different ways. Mothers face the steepest overall reduction in social time. Fathers often retain slightly more leisure hours but spend them in isolated activities: screen time, solo exercise, rather than connection. In both cases, the social network contracts. The challenge is not finding more time. It is using the limited time that remains for connection rather than recovery alone.

sections.h2: Parenting contexts are full of potential friends
paragraphs: Baby groups, playground regulars, pediatrician waiting rooms, parent-teacher gatherings, postnatal classes, these are some of the densest concentrations of people in the same situation you will find as an adult. Everyone is tired. Everyone is figuring it out. Nobody expects you to be polished.
The shared context does a lot of the conversational work for you. You already have something in common. You do not need to manufacture small talk from nothing. The baby is the opening.
Apps like Mush or Peanut exist specifically to help new parents meet each other. They are worth using alongside physical contexts, not instead of them.
sections.quote: Most adult loneliness is not about missing people. It is about missing the repetition that turns people into friends.

sections.h2: The baby is the opening. You have to do the rest.
paragraphs: The shared context lowers the barrier to conversation, but it does not create a friendship on its own. You still have to follow up.

After a warm exchange at the playground or a baby group, send a short message the same day or the next day. Mention something specific from the conversation. Suggest one small thing: a coffee, a walk, meeting again at the same time next week.

Keep the ask low-stakes and practical. 'Would you want to do the park again Thursday?' is easier to say yes to than 'we should definitely get together.' One concrete invitation beats ten friendly conversations that go nowhere.

cultureRef.body: In Dead to Me, Jen and Judy meet in a grief support group, both overwhelmed and low on bandwidth. What works between them is not polish or patience. It is radical honesty from the start, cutting through the social performance that usually precedes real friendship. For parents who are already exhausted, the same principle applies. The warmth from a playground exchange is enough to act on. You do not need to wait until you have enough energy to be impressive.

sections.h2: Lower the bar for what the friendship looks like early on
paragraphs: Friendship with a baby in the picture is not going to look like the friendships you had before. Plans will be interrupted. Someone will cancel. The baby will cry at the wrong moment. That is all normal.

A short, messy, cut-short coffee is still contact. A walk where both of you are half-distracted still counts. The goal in the early phase is not depth. It is repetition. Depth comes later, once the connection has had enough room to grow.
If you wait for the conditions to be right, for nap times to align, for the baby to be in a better phase, for you to feel less exhausted, you may wait for years.

sections.h2: Not every match will stick. That is fine.
paragraphs: Some people you meet through parenting contexts will become real friends. Others will remain pleasant acquaintances you see at the playground. A few will not click at all.
This is not failure. It is the normal ratio. What matters is that you keep putting yourself in contexts where repetition can happen, and that you follow up when something feels warm.

You are not looking for the one perfect person. You are building a small, functional social life in a period of life when that takes real effort. For the mechanics of following up once a conversation felt warm, see how to follow up after meeting someone. And if your existing friendships are also struggling since the baby arrived, how to stay friends with someone who just had a baby shows what your non-parent friends are navigating.

faq.q: How do I make mom friends as a new parent?
faq.a: Show up consistently in places where other parents are, baby groups, playgrounds, postnatal classes, and follow up when a conversation feels warm. Send a short message the same day referencing something specific from your exchange, and suggest one small, low-stakes next step. Repetition and follow-through do most of the work.

faq.q: How do I meet other parents when I have a newborn?
faq.a: Postnatal classes and baby groups are the most reliable starting point because they create repeated contact with the same people over several weeks. Pediatrician waiting rooms and local playgrounds are also good contexts. Apps like Peanut or Mush can supplement in-person contact but work best alongside it, not instead of it.

faq.q: What if we only have the baby in common?
faq.a: That is enough to begin with. Most friendships in a new context start from one shared thing. As you spend more time together, other things emerge. If after a few meetings you genuinely have nothing else in common, that is useful information, but most people find the overlap is wider than they expected once the baby stage gives them space to talk.

faq.q: How do I follow up with another parent when I am exhausted?
faq.a: Keep the message short. You do not need energy for a long, thoughtful note. Something like 'It was great meeting you today. Would you want to do the park again Thursday?' takes thirty seconds and does all the work. Low energy is the condition, not a reason to wait.

faq.q: Is it normal to feel lonely as a new parent?
faq.a: Extremely common. New parenthood disrupts the routines and contexts that sustained your social life before. The friendships you had may not fit the new version of your life. This is structural, not a sign that something is wrong with you. It is also solvable, but it requires building a new social structure on purpose, not waiting for the old one to come back.

faq.q: What if I feel like I need more than a guide? Is friendship coaching an option?
faq.a: Yes. Friendship coaching is practical support for adults who want help turning good intentions into action, meeting people, following up, and building real connection in a specific season of life. New parenthood is one of the transitions it is most useful for. See what is friendship coaching for more.

faq.q: Does this apply to dads too? How do I make friends as a new dad?
faq.a: The challenge is the same regardless of gender, the social structures have disappeared and the energy is low. The cultural expectation that men should not need to reach out for new friendship can add an extra layer of awkwardness, but the approach is identical: find recurring contexts where other parents are, follow up when something feels warm, and keep the invitation low-stakes. The barrier is not a skill gap. It is the first move.

guides.title: How to Keep Work Friendships After Going Remote | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: When you go remote, you lose the hallway moments that kept work friendships alive. Here is how to replace that structure before the friendships quietly fade.
guides.h1: How to keep work friendships after going remote
guides.lead: Most work friendships are built on proximity and shared context. When you go remote, both disappear. The friendship does not have to go with them, but without deliberate effort, it usually does.
guides.intro: Keeping work friendships after going remote is different from maintaining any other friendship. The relationship formed in a context that no longer exists. Both people have to decide, usually without saying so, whether to move it somewhere else. Most never make that decision. The friendship fades, and nobody is sure who let it go.
guides.authorNote: I have spoken to many people who went remote and lost friendships they genuinely valued. Not because the other person stopped caring, but because the office had been doing most of the maintenance. The ones who kept those friendships did one thing differently: they created a new structure.
guides.pullQuote: Going remote is a useful test for work friendships. It removes the infrastructure and shows you which connections had something underneath it. The ones that do are worth a little deliberate effort to keep.

sections.h2: The office was doing the work. Now you have to.
paragraphs: Work friendships run on repeated, unplanned contact: the coffee run, the walk to a meeting, the five minutes at the start of a call when the agenda has not started yet. None of it felt like effort because none of it was. It just happened.

Remote work removes all of that. What remains is a relationship that was sustained by a structure that no longer exists. Both people are busy. Both people have adjusted to working without the incidental contact. And so the friendship quietly stops.
Understanding this is the first useful step. You are not bad at friendship. The infrastructure that was doing the work for you has been removed. The question is whether you want to rebuild it.

Social Presence Theory, developed by communication researchers in the 1970s, describes how physical co-presence creates a kind of ambient social closeness that is difficult to fully replicate digitally. Scheduled video calls and message threads do not substitute for the low-stakes, unplanned contact of shared physical space. That gap is not a failure of technology. It is a structural limitation that remote friendships have to consciously work around.

cultureRef.body: In The Intern, Jules and Ben's connection builds through daily proximity, small observations, and a steady stream of moments that nobody scheduled. He sees her under pressure. She sees him steady. When that shared space disappears, so does the effortless insight into each other's days. What was once automatic has to become deliberate: a proactive check-in, a question you would have asked in the hallway, attention that used to happen without anyone trying.

sections.h2: Decide which friendships you actually want to keep
paragraphs: Not every work friendship needs to survive the transition. Some were sustained entirely by proximity and had no real substance beyond that. That is fine. Trying to maintain every office relationship will exhaust you and dilute the effort you put into the ones that matter.

Think about which people you genuinely liked, not just which ones you saw most. Who are the people you thought about when something interesting happened? Who did you miss in the first weeks of working from home? Those are the friendships worth investing in.
A short list of two or three people you actually want to maintain a real connection with is more useful than a long list of polite check-ins with everyone.

sections.h2: Create a structure to replace the one that disappeared
paragraphs: The most reliable way to keep a work friendship alive after going remote is to create a recurring touchpoint: a monthly virtual coffee, a shared lunch on video once in a while, a standing message thread that is not about work. It does not have to be formal. It just has to exist and repeat.

One-off plans that rely on someone remembering to suggest them tend to drift. A recurring slot removes the friction of constantly re-initiating. If it feels like too much to suggest a recurring call, start with one specific plan and see if it leads naturally to another.

The goal is contact often enough that the friendship does not have to be rebuilt from scratch each time. Once a month is usually enough. Once every three months is the minimum before it starts to feel like starting over.
sections.quote: A friendship does not need to be intense to stay alive. It needs to be consistent.

sections.h2: The real question: work friend or actual friend?
paragraphs: Going remote is a useful test. It forces both people to decide whether the friendship exists on its own terms or only in the context of work.

Some work friendships, when moved outside the office, reveal that you have genuine things in common: curiosity, values, humor, life situations. Those friendships can become something more durable than what you had before.

Others reveal that the shared context was doing most of the work. That is not a failure. It is useful information. You can appreciate what the friendship was without putting energy into something that was always more circumstantial than chosen.

cultureRef.body: In Call My Agent, Mathias and Andrea spend years as rivals inside the same talent agency. They compete for clients, undermine each other's deals, and clash on almost every decision. What eventually transforms the rivalry into something durable is not warmth. It is the accumulation of high-stakes situations where each one keeps choosing to rely on the other's judgment. Friendship born from professional friction inside a shared system is not the warmest version. But it is often the most loyal.

sections.h2: If they stayed in the office and you went remote
paragraphs: Asymmetric remote arrangements are particularly hard on work friendships. The person still in the office has incidental contact with other colleagues; you do not. The friendship can start to feel like you are the only one making effort, because structurally, you are.

Name it lightly if you need to. Something like 'I know I miss the spontaneous stuff more than you probably do' acknowledges the asymmetry without making it a complaint. And then make one concrete suggestion for how to stay connected in a way that works for both of you.

If the other person is also making some effort, even small effort, the friendship can survive and sometimes deepen. If the effort is entirely one-sided over time, that is information too. For building an entirely new social life as a remote worker, see how to make friends when you work from home. For general maintenance principles that apply to any adult friendship, how to keep friends as an adult covers it directly.

faq.q: How do I stay in touch with coworkers after going remote?
faq.a: Create a recurring touchpoint, a monthly call, a standing virtual coffee, a message thread that is not about work tasks. The goal is contact that happens regularly without requiring constant re-initiation. One specific standing plan is more reliable than open invitations that depend on someone remembering to suggest them.

faq.q: Is it weird to reach out to work friends outside of work after going remote?
faq.a: Not at all. If you were genuinely close, a message that is clearly personal rather than work-related is almost always welcomed. Keep the first reach-out simple: mention you miss the incidental contact and suggest one specific thing, like a call or a catch-up.

faq.q: How do I keep friendships with people still in the office when I work remotely?
faq.a: Acknowledge the asymmetry and work around it. They have built-in contact with other people; you do not. That means you will probably need to initiate more than they do, at least at first. Suggest a recurring touchpoint that fits both schedules and is explicitly social rather than work-related.

faq.q: Why do work friendships fade when you go remote?
faq.a: Because most work friendships run on proximity and incidental contact, the coffee run, the walk between meetings, the informal conversations that happen without planning. Remote work removes all of that infrastructure. Without it, contact stops unless someone deliberately creates new structure to replace it.

faq.q: How often should I check in with work friends when I am remote?
faq.a: Once a month is usually enough to keep a friendship alive without it feeling like maintenance. Once every three months is the minimum before each contact starts to feel like restarting from scratch. More than twice a month is only necessary if the friendship has genuine depth and both people are investing in it.

guides.title: How to Make New Friends After a Friendship Ends | The Friendship Practice
guides.description: Losing a close friend leaves a real void. Clearing out a broken connection makes room for something healthier. Here is how to rebuild with confidence.
guides.h1: How to make new friends after a friendship ends
guides.lead: When an important friendship ends, whether it broke suddenly or faded over months, the gap it leaves is real. Rebuilding is possible. But it rarely works the same way as making friends did before, and understanding why that is makes the process easier.
guides.intro: Making friends after a significant friendship ends is not the same as making friends from scratch. You are doing it while carrying a loss, often with a lower appetite for the vulnerability that new friendship requires. This is not weakness. It is the normal aftermath of losing something that mattered.
guides.authorNote: Friendship loss is one of the most under-acknowledged forms of grief in adult life. There is no ceremony for it, no language for it, rarely anyone who asks how you are doing. But losing someone who was part of your daily life and your sense of being known is a real loss. The work of rebuilding starts with naming it as such.
guides.pullQuote: Friendship loss is rarely given the name grief, but that is what it is. And rebuilding after it works better when you start from where you actually are, carrying the loss, rather than pretending it is not there.

sections.h2: Give the loss its proper name
paragraphs: Friendship loss is rarely acknowledged as grief the way that romantic or family loss is. There are no rituals for it, no common language, few people who think to ask how you are doing about a friendship that ended.

But the impact is real. Someone who was part of your daily life, your inner reference points, your sense of being known, that absence is significant. Giving it a name before moving on is not self-indulgent. It is what makes the next step feel grounded rather than forced.

Naming the loss for what it is, not minimizing it, not dramatizing it, just acknowledging that it matters, is the useful first step. Not because you need to process it fully before you can move forward, but because skipping it tends to show up later as low motivation, excessive caution, or a vague reluctance to try.

Research on relational resilience has found that people who acknowledge loss explicitly, who name what happened and what it meant rather than minimizing or bypassing it, tend to rebuild more successfully afterward. The absence of social ritual around friendship endings does not make the grief smaller. It makes it harder to locate, name, and move through.

sections.h2: You are not looking for a replacement
paragraphs: One of the quieter obstacles to making new friends after a loss is the unconscious comparison. A new person does not know the things your old friend knew. They do not have the shared history. They do not fit into your life the same way.
They are not supposed to. The goal is not to find someone who fills the same role. The goal is to find people who can become meaningful over time, in their own way, starting from where you are now.

This sounds obvious but is easy to lose sight of when every new connection is measured against what you had. The comparison is understandable, but it tends to make everything feel like a disappointment before it has had a chance to grow into something.

sections.h2: Start before you feel fully ready
paragraphs: The confidence to build new friendships often returns only after you have started building them, not before. Waiting until you feel ready can become a form of permanent avoidance.

This does not mean forcing yourself into social situations that feel wrong. It means making one small move, going to a place where you can see the same people more than once, following up after a warm conversation, before you feel entirely ready for it.

Early contact does not have to be deep or easy. It just has to happen. The emotional residue from the loss becomes more manageable when you have evidence, however small, that connection is still possible.
sections.quote: You do not need a more charismatic personality. You need a more deliberate practice.

sections.h2: Use what the loss taught you
paragraphs: The end of an important friendship often leaves you with clearer knowledge of what you actually want from friendship: the kind of reciprocity that matters to you, the communication style that fits you, the qualities you need in someone to feel genuinely close.

That clarity is hard-earned and worth using. It does not mean screening every new person against a list of requirements. It means being a little less willing to invest in connections where the fit feels obviously wrong, and a little more deliberate about following through with the ones that feel genuinely warm.
People who have lost a close friendship are often better at friendship than before, not because the loss improved them, but because it taught them what they actually value.

cultureRef.body: In Wine Country, a group of friends in their late forties gather for a birthday trip. They spend much of it confronting how much has shifted since their twenties. Some of the closeness they assumed was still intact turns out to be sentiment about who they once were together. Letting go of a version of a friendship that no longer fits, even when the affection is real, clears the space to build something that fits your current life instead of your past one.

sections.h2: New friendship after loss builds differently
paragraphs: Friendships you build after a significant loss tend to form more deliberately. Less accidental, less dependent on circumstance, more consciously chosen. That is not a deficit. It is a different kind of foundation.

You may find you are more direct about making plans, more attentive to whether the effort is mutual, more willing to say what the friendship means to you. These are not signs of neediness. They are signs that you know what you are doing and why.

The friendships that grow from this place can become some of the more durable ones you have. Not because they are built on loss, but because they are built on clarity. To understand the ending itself, or to decide whether reconnection is worth trying, see when a friendship ends and how to reconnect with old friends.

faq.q: How do I make new friends after losing a close friend?
faq.a: Start small and before you feel ready. Choose one recurring context where you can see the same people more than once, follow up after warm interactions, and accept that early connection will feel different from the friendship you lost. The goal is not to replace what you had but to build something new from where you are.

faq.q: Is it normal to feel lonely after a friendship ends?
faq.a: Very. Friendship loss is a real form of grief, even if it is rarely named as one. The absence of someone who was part of your daily life and sense of being known leaves a genuine gap. Feeling lonely after that is not a sign something is wrong with you. It is a proportionate response to a real loss.

faq.q: How long does it take to get over losing a best friend?
faq.a: There is no fixed timeline, and getting over it is the wrong frame. The loss becomes less acute over time, especially as new connections grow. But the process is not linear and does not follow a schedule. What tends to help is not waiting for the feeling to pass before acting, but starting to build new connection while the feeling is still there.

faq.q: How do I rebuild my social life after a friendship breakup?
faq.a: The same way you build any social life: find contexts where repeated contact is possible, follow up when something feels warm, and invest in the connections that have genuine reciprocity. The difference after a loss is that you are doing it with less energy and more caution than usual. Adjust the scale accordingly, smaller steps, lower stakes, more patience with yourself.

faq.q: Should I try to repair the friendship or move on?
faq.a: That depends on why it ended. If the ending was mutual and clear, or if the relationship was genuinely harmful, moving on is usually the right direction. If it faded rather than broke, and the connection still feels worth something to you, a simple, low-stakes message is worth trying. Most people who reach out after a drift are welcomed. Most people who wait for the other person to move first wait indefinitely.

faq.q: Can a friendship coach help with this?
faq.a: Yes. Rebuilding a social life after a significant friendship ends is exactly the kind of situation friendship coaching is useful for: you know what you want, but following through feels harder than usual. See what is friendship coaching to understand what that support actually involves.
